text: The surgeon begins the procedure by dissecting the pedicle of a large calcified subserous fibroid, revealing its intramural component. The dissection continues with the release of additional intramural and subserous fibroids as identified on the MRI.

The surgeon performs ovarial lysis by releasing endometriotic adhesions along the tubal margins and the surface of the ovaries. Bilateral endometriosis is visible, along with a small component of deep endometriosis, which has been completely released. The pouch of Douglas, previously obliterated, is now fully released.

The surgeon infiltrates the uterine tissue with vasopressin before proceeding with the dissection of posterior submucous, subserous, and intramural fibroids. Three fibroids are released from the posterior aspect of the uterus. Bipolar current is applied gently to control bleeding and maintain the dissection plane without compromising uterine musculature.
The surgeon sutures the myoma beds on the anterior and posterior aspects of the uterus using a quill suture in multiple layers, ensuring the cavity is obliterated to prevent hematoma formation.

The surgeon introduces multiple fibroids into a specialized containment bag designed for safe morcellation, preventing spillage into the peritoneal cavity. The morcellator is inserted through the lateral end of the bag to morcellate the fibroids, ensuring containment and facilitating histopathological examination. The surgical team carefully manages the position of the fibroids to prevent them from slipping into the paracolic gutters during the procedure.
After completing the morcellation of the fibroids, the surgeon proceeds to close the surgical ports, marking the end of the procedure.
